title: Chapter Six: Afterimage
content: I walk.

Not toward home — home has mirrors and I can't look at myself right now, can't meet the eyes of whatever face is looking back, can't track which version of me is wearing the skin today. So I walk south, through the park entrance at 59th, past the Plaza with its lit windows and its doormen who nod at nothing because I'm already past them, already dissolving into the stream of tourists and pretzel carts and the man selling hot dogs who calls me "lady" without hesitation.
Lady. Sure. Tonight I'll take it.

The city blurs. Not tears — I'm not crying, I don't cry, haven't cried since Seville in 1732 and even then it was rain on my face and I let people assume. The blur is something else. A kind of double vision. The park path beneath my boots is here, is now, is 2026, but underneath it: cobblestones. Underneath the sodium lights: candlelight. Underneath the traffic on Central Park South: the lap of water against stone, against wood, against a gondola's hull.
Underneath everything: a dying man's breath.

I touch the base of my neck. My fingers find the spot just above the collarbone, the hollow where the sternum begins, the place where Rowan's fingers rested for three seconds during the fitting. Three seconds. I counted. My body counted. My body counts everything now, every point of contact, every shift in the air pressure of a room when someone moves closer. I press the spot and there's nothing there — of course there's nothing there — but the ghost of the touch persists like a stain, like wine on linen, like blood on a handkerchief.
I press harder. Did the sensation belong to now or to then? I can't tell. The hum won't let me tell.

The wind comes off the park, sharp and November, stripping the last of the oak leaves from the branches overhead. I feel it on my face, on my bare hands, cold enough to sting. Good. I hold onto the sting. The sting is now.

A pretzel cart. I buy coffee without looking at the vendor. Bitter, cold — it's been sitting, and I take a long sip anyway, and the taste is burnt and sour and so aggressively ordinary that it almost works, almost anchors me, almost drags me back into the world of bad coffee and overpriced cart snacks and a Saturday night in Manhattan where nothing supernatural ever happens to anyone except me.
Almost.

The fountain rises ahead. Bethesda. The angel with her hands raised, water falling around her in sheets. I can hear it from thirty feet away — that bright, continuous sound, water over stone, water over water. It's the opposite of the sound in my head, which is a wet sound, a failing sound, the sound of lungs filling with fluid that should not be there.
I sit on a bench. The iron is cold through my trousers. I hold the coffee cup between both hands and I breathe.

*The oar slips. Marco reaches across and adjusts my grip — his hand over mine, patient, his skin warm from the sun. "Gentle," he says. "The canal is not your enemy." The Grand Canal is glass in the morning light. I am Orsino. I am twenty-three in this body, or two hundred and twelve, depending on how you count. Marco doesn't know. Marco only knows the man in the boat with him, the one who rows badly and kisses well.*
The memory cracks open and I'm inside it, not watching but *there*, the sun on the back of my neck, the smell of canal water and Marco's bay rum, and then—

A cough. Not in the memory. *Behind* the memory. A different year, a different season. The cough that started in autumn and didn't stop until spring. The handkerchief pressed to his mouth, linen white pressing and lifting red, pressing and lifting red, over and over, and the sound — wet, thick, the sound of a body giving up one system at a time, surrendering territory inch by territory inch, the lungs first and then the blood and then the will.
I put my face in my hands. The coffee tips. I don't catch it. It spills across the bench and pools on the stone and I don't care.
A jogger passes. Nikes on the path. AirPods in. She doesn't see me. Why would she? I'm just a woman on a bench in Central Park on a Saturday night, face in her hands, nothing to see.
My world is not normal.
I stay on the bench until the fountain sounds like a fountain again and not like breathing. Until the coffee on the ground is just coffee. Until I can stand up without my knees shaking.
Then I walk. Not home. Never home tonight. Downtown, toward the archive.

Sable's archive occupies the third floor of a townhouse on East 13th that looks from the outside like every other townhouse on the block — limestone façade, black iron railings, a window box with dead geraniums that Sable maintains specifically because live ones would draw attention. The door unlocks with a key she gave me in 1952. I've never lost it. I've lost everything else — names, faces, centuries — but not the key.

The stairs creak. They always creak. Sable says she keeps them that way so she knows when someone's coming, but I think she keeps them that way because she's sentimental in all the places she denies being sentimental.

The archive stretches across the entire floor: floor-to-ceiling drawers, climate-controlled, each one labeled in Sable's small precise hand. Dynasty. Era. Body. The cartography of a life that won't hold still.

I don't need to read the labels. I know where the Venetian drawer is. Third row, second from the left, between *Florence 1582* and *Mantua 1610*. My fingers find the brass pull and the drawer slides open on silent runners and there it is.
The silk.

Dark blue, almost black, shot through with silver thread that catches the overhead light. I wore this as Orsino. I wore this to Marco's funeral. I wore this in the gondola that carried his body to San Michele, the island of the dead, and the silk was stiff with rain and I was numb with something beyond grief, something that had no name because names are for experiences that have ended and this one hadn't ended, has never ended, is ending still, right now, in this room.
I lift the silk from the drawer.

The grief is immediate. Physical. Like pressing on a bruise, like pressing your thumb into a wound to confirm it's still there, still open, still refusing to scar over. I hold the fabric to my face and breathe.
Lavender.

Four hundred years and lavender persists. I packed the silk in dried lavender after the funeral — I couldn't wash it, couldn't bring myself to dissolve what remained of that day, so I folded it around sprigs of lavender and sealed it in a cedar box and for four hundred years the lavender has held, faint but undeniable, a ghost of a scent, a fingerprint of a morning when I stood on a dock and watched a boat carry away the only person who ever knew me completely.

My knees give. I catch myself on the drawer's edge. The silk is still pressed to my face and I'm breathing into it like it's oxygen and maybe it is, maybe grief is its own atmosphere, maybe I've been suffocating in normal air for four centuries.
"You're hiding."

Sable. The doorway. I didn't hear her come in because I was breathing lavender and canal water and trying not to drown. She stands with one shoulder against the frame, arms crossed, watching me the way she watches everything — with the forensic calm of someone who catalogs damage for a living.
"I'm not hiding," I say. The silk is against my chest now, bunched in my fist. "I needed to touch something real."
"Real." She says the word like she's testing its weight. "And the silk is real? Or the memory attached to it?"
"Both."
Sable crosses the room. She doesn't touch me — she knows better, has always known better — but she sits in the chair opposite mine and leans forward, elbows on knees, and waits.
So I tell her.

I tell her about the fitting. About Rowan's hands taking the measurements, professional, precise. About the hum rising, the way it always rises when I'm being seen — really seen, not glanced at, not categorized, but *looked at* by someone who's paying attention. I tell her about the moment his fingers touched the base of my neck, the hollow above the collarbone, and the hum became a roar, and Venice came flooding back like a burst pipe, like a canal overflowing its banks, and I couldn't breathe, couldn't stay, couldn't—

I stop. I don't tell her about Rowan's face. About the way he looked at me when I pulled away — not confused, not offended, but *seeing*, as if the crack in my composure had shown him something true. That part is too raw. That part I keep.
Sable absorbs all of it. She doesn't interrupt, doesn't offer comfort. She's not sentimental. She's forensic. When I finish, she sits with it for a long moment, and then she says:
"The last time you felt the hum this strongly was 1867."
A cold finger trails down my spine.
"Right before the Thornfield shift," she continues. "You became Ora. You lost forty years of memory. You forgot your own name for three weeks."
"I remember."
"Do you?" Sable's eyes are steady. Dark. Unblinking. "Because you're doing the same thing again. Getting close to someone. Letting the hum build. Waiting for your body to detonate."
"I'm not getting close to anyone." The words come out sharp, defensive, and I hate how they sound. "It's a commission. A suit. That's all."

"A suit." Sable repeats it without inflection. The lack of inflection is worse than sarcasm would be. "The same way Marco was a landlord. The same way William was a colleague. The same way every seismic shift in your life has started with someone you swore was incidental."
"Stop."
"You asked me to keep the archive because I don't lie to you. I'm not going to start now."
The silence between us is its own kind of terrain — rocky, unlit, easy to break an ankle on. I look down at the silk in my hands. The silver thread glints. Outside, a siren passes, fades.
"I'm not Thornfield," I say. "I'm not twenty-three anymore. I know what the hum is now. I know what it does."
"Then why are you sitting in my archive at nine p.m. holding a funeral shroud?"
I flinch. It's a small motion, barely visible, but Sable sees it. Sable sees everything.

She stands. Crosses to the drawer I left open — the Venetian drawer, the silk's home — and reaches beneath the shelf. Her fingers find a second pull, hidden at the back. A drawer I've never noticed in sixty years of coming here.
"You didn't know about this one," she says. Not a question.
"What is it?"
Sable draws the drawer out. Inside: a single object. A letter, sealed in wax, resting on a bed of black velvet like an artifact in a museum. Which, I suppose, it is.
The wax is deep red. Cracked with age. The cracks are fine, web-like — the slow fracturing of centuries, not the violent break of a hasty seal. Whatever's inside has been inside for a very long time.

"You brought this to me in 1943," Sable says. Her voice is quiet now. Not gentle — Sable is never gentle — but precise, measured, the way a surgeon's voice is measured before they tell you what the scan revealed. "You were in London. The bombs. You came to my flat at three in the morning, shaking, and you handed me this and you said —"
She pauses. Looking at me. Giving me the chance to finish it myself.
I can't.
"You said: *Don't let me open this until I'm ready to stop running.*"

The hum in my chest goes quiet. Not silent — never silent — but quiet, the way the ocean goes quiet when you're standing too deep in it, when the water closes over your ears and everything becomes muffled and vast and distant.

I know what's inside the letter. I wrote it. I wrote it to myself in 1867, after Thornfield, after the shift that stole forty years and left me blank and gasping in a strange room in a strange body with a strange name I didn't recognize on a stranger's lips. After William — my William, my colleague, my almost-lover, my ruin — looked at what I'd become and called me an abomination. After I ran. After I ran and ran and ran, and then I stopped running long enough to find ink and paper and to write the truth down, the whole truth, the truth I was too afraid to carry but too bereft to discard.

I folded the truth into an envelope. I sealed it in wax. I kept it for twenty years before I gave it to Sable, because I knew — I *knew* — that if I kept it myself I would open it in a moment of weakness and the weakness would destroy me. So I gave it to the one person in the world who would not hand it back no matter how hard I asked.

Sable slides the drawer closed but doesn't lock it. She steps away. She leaves the letter on its velvet bed, visible through the glass panel she's replaced the drawer front with — clear, protective, keeping the air out and the century in.
"I'm not asking you to open it tonight," she says. She's at the door now, half-turned, giving me the privacy she knows I won't take. "I'm asking you to remember that it exists."
She goes. Her footsteps on the creaking stairs, fading.

I sit in the archive with the Venetian silk still clutched in one hand and the letter behind glass in front of me, deep red wax cracked like a wound that never healed, and the hum rising again, slow and steady, the tide coming back in.

The self I was then — 1867, broken, running, writing to a future she couldn't imagine — reaches across a hundred and fifty-nine years, and the hand she extends is the letter, and the letter is sealed, and I am not ready.
But the letter is there.
And so is the hum.
